learning_outcomes.description: Understand the nature of overarching issues in academic writing which often cause problems for students who use English as a second language.

Demonstrate the ability to convey ideas and information clearly and fluently in written form through written assessment, including through the appropriate use of digital tools and technologies that support academic writing.
Demonstrate the ability to collect, evaluate, analyse and organise information from scholarly and digital sources for use in academic writing tasks.

Demonstrate critical thinking skills by identifying, analysing, and revising issues in academic writing, including evaluating AI-generated or AI-assisted text for accuracy, coherence, and appropriateness.
Demonstrate knowledge of ethics and ethical standards in academic writing, especially as they pertain to conducting research.
Demonstrate the acquisition of scholarly research and writing skills which enable students to engage critically with academic literature and contribute meaningfully to academic discussions.

Demonstrate the ability to interact and collaborate effectively with others in academic contexts, including peer review and collaborative writing activities, to achieve shared learning and writing goals.

assessments.assessment_title: In-class Performance: Finding and Evaluating Sources
assessments.due_date:
24/08/2026 - 27/08/2026

This is an in-class assessment during WEEK 5.
assessments.task_description:
In this in-class assessment, you will need to search for sources via the UQ Library website and provide two peer-reviewed journal articles that you may use in your comparative article review (i.e. Assessment 2). You will also need to evaluate how reliable and useful these articles are for your topic, based on the skills and instructions covered in Module 3 of the course.

This performance activity will take place during the tutorial activities in WEEK 5.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Select two papers that are examples of excellent and important work in your discipline / research area. These should be ideally the ones that you have identified in Assessment 1. Choose journal articles (not books or other genres). 

Then, select one paper that is clearly outside of your discipline / research area. For example, if you are in Arts & Humanities (A&H), select 2 A&H papers and 1 Physical Science paper. If you are in the Physical Sciences (PS), choose 2 PS papers and 1 A&H paper, etc. Choose journal articles (not books or other genres). 

Then, complete BOTH A) AND B) BELOW

PLEASE WRITE IN PARAGRAPHS (NOT BULLET POINTS)

A) Individual reviews of research papers (500 words for each paper = 1500 words total)

  • Give the full reference of the work in the style typically used in your discipline
  • Write a short paragraph outlining the key points/findings of the paper (no more than 100 words).
  • For these papers - Explain what you can learn about writing in your discipline and the other discipline by reading these papers. Questions you need to ask yourself are:
  • How do I know the papers are from my disciplinary area? or not from my disciplinary area?
  • What is special about the structure and language features of these papers? Can you describe them?
  • What referencing style is used?
  • Do they make particular use of quotes, data, charts, statistics, etc.?
  • What is the general tone (academic/general), level of formality (formal/informal), use of author intrusion (e.g. self-mention) as well as reader engagement (e.g. attitude markers)?
  • What kind of claims do the authors make, and how do they make them? Are they generally strong claims? Do they tend to make more cautious claims?

B) Comparative essay

Essay of between 750–1000 words that does the following:

  • Summarise how the writing in the two papers from your discipline is different from the writing in the paper outside of your discipline. You need to demonstrate that as a result of reading these papers and analysing them, that you are fully aware of disciplinary writing practices. Don't just repeat the information from A) above.
  • Reflect on how your new knowledge of disciplinary writing practices has changed the way you think about writing for your discipline in the future. You should explain:
  • Was there something you were doing in your writing before that was not appropriate for writing in your discipline?
  • What is the most important thing you are going to change for your writing in the future as a result of doing this assessment?

Submit to the Turnitin link in the Assessment folder on Blackboard. Submit A and B in the SAME FILE.

Generative AI & MT Statement:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.due_date:
In-class pitch idea sessions - (P/F Hurdle) - WEEK 9 & WEEK 10 - 21/09/2026 - 8/10/2026

IMRD Paper - WEEK 12, Friday 2:00 pm - 23/10/2026
assessments.task_description:
There are 2 Parts in this assessment:

Part 1: In-class Pitch idea sessions

This component of the assessment is a PASS/FAIL hurdle. Participation in at least one of the two Pitching Ideas sessions is a mandatory hurdle requirement. Students who do not satisfy this hurdle requirement may receive a maximum final grade of 3 for the course, regardless of marks achieved in other assessment components, including the Mini-Research Paper.

The topic of your idea pitch and mini-research paper should be of your own choice, provided that it is a genuine academic topic and is relevant to your discipline.

In WEEK 9 and WEEK 10 of the semester, you will pitch your IMRD paper ideas during class. You may use handwritten notes or PowerPoint slides when presenting your ideas in small groups. Ideally, in WEEK 9, you will present your general research topic, relevant literature, identified research gaps, and research questions. In WEEK 10, you will continue by presenting your proposed methodology and tentative discussion.

During these sessions, you will be required to complete peer-review assessment forms, and you may use the feedback and notes from these activities when writing your IMRD paper.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This part of the assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted during the delivery of the pitch presentation. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Part 2: Written IMRD Paper

Following the pitch idea sessions in class, you are required to write your IMRD paper.

The paper will be in the IMRD format - Introduction, Methods, Results and Discussion (as in Module 7 of the course). The mini-research paper is on a topic of your choice, although it should be a real academic topic, and should be from your own discipline.

You do not have to collect data or find participants - you can make up/pretend you have the participants for your methods section, and the data for your results section.

You must include appropriate citations and references for your 'paper'.  Referencing format should be the one primarily used in your discipline - e.g. APA, Chicago, MLA, Harvard - you will need to check with your disciplinary tutors about which format is used in your discipline. References do not count as part of the word count.

Your written assessment must be new work that you have written, please do not submit previously completed work to avoid self-plagiarism.

The IMRD paper should be no longer than 1,500 words.

Upload this document via Turnitin using the link in the relevant 'assessment' folder. 

----------------

DETAILED UPLOAD INSTRUCTIONS:

  1. Submit Word or PDF file of research paper to the link on a Blackboard folder under Assessment.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.due_date:
9/11/2026 2:00 pm

Weekly Extend Completion is due throughout the semester, and Extend Reflection Video is due in Exam Week 1.
assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.task_description:
This course is built around an online course hosted on UQ's Extend online learning platform. This online course contains a wide range of activities that will help you to learn about (and improve) your academic writing. These activities are available via WEEKLY MODULES and you can complete them online at your own time.

This assessment is in TWO PARTS.

PART 1: Extend Completion

This is a PASS/FAIL hurdle for the course

You are required to complete at least 80% of the online Extend activities to meet the course hurdle requirement. Students who do not satisfy this hurdle requirement may receive a maximum final grade of 3 for the course, regardless of marks achieved in other assessment tasks/components.

There are a total of 13 weekly online modules available from the beginning of the semester. Students are advised to complete each module in sequence, during the corresponding teaching week (e.g. Module 1 in Week 1, Module 2 in Week 2). Each weekly online module may take between 1 and 2 hours to complete, depending on the content covered via different activity types, including problem questions (multiple choice, drop-down menus, checkboxes), video completion (entire videos must be watched), discussion forums (following all instructions), peer instruction questions (including minimum character requirements), drag-and-drop activities, and annotation tasks (see online modules for details).

You can track your activity completion rate through the ‘Progress’ tab in the Extend course (instructions will be provided online and during the Week 1 tutorial).

IMPORTANT: You do not need to answer all activities correctly. You only need to complete them. Incorrect responses are acceptable, and you will have opportunities to revise your answers after receiving feedback.

PART 2: Extend Reflection

You must produce a Video Reflection explaining (1) how you drew upon the knowledge gained from the WRIT6001 course content to support your writing in the course assessments (e.g. the Comparative Article Review and/or the IMRD Paper) with detailed examples, and (2) how you will transfer and apply this knowledge to your writing in the future. This video reflection should be approximately 5 minutes long.

The presentations will be recorded as a full video which shows your face in full screen (No PPTs are allowed). You may use any video recording software you wish as long as you have access to the original video file for upload. These videos must be uploaded to Blackboard, and instructions will be provided in that folder and by your tutor prior to the deadline.

  1. At the START of the video, you must talk to the camera (i.e. NOT a photograph) and introduce yourself clearly showing your UQ ID card on FULL SCREEN. If you do not have a UQ ID card, please use a passport or valid Australian driver’s license with your name in English. If the marker cannot see your ID clearly, your video will be considered not submitted.
  2. Attach a screenshot of your Extend completion rate in Part 1 (i.e. at least 80% of Extend Participation) to the same submission folder. Please note that course coordinator and graders will also cross-check your Extend completion when marking your Extend reflection video.

DETAILED UPLOAD INSTRUCTIONS:

  1. Submit your video to the relevant link on a Blackboard folder under Assessment.
  2. Submit a screenshot of your Extend Completion to the same folder.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. 

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
